name: __chkfeat
description: Checks for hardware features at runtime using the CHKFEAT hint instruction. `__chkfeat` returns a bitmask where a bit is set if the same bit in the input argument is set and the corresponding feature is enabled. It can be used with predefined macros:  | **Macro name**           | **Value**           | **Meaning**                                        | | ``_CHKFEAT_GCS``         | 1                   | Guarded Control Stack (GCS) protection is enabled. |  Note: for usability reasons the return value differs from how the CHKFEAT instruction sets X16.

name: __cls
description: Returns the number of leading sign bits in `x`. When `x` is zero it returns the argument width - 1, that is, 31 or 63. These intrinsics are available on all targets. On targets without the CLZ instruction it should be implemented as an instruction sequence or a call to such a sequence. Fast hardware implementation (using a CLS instruction or a short code sequence involving the CLZ instruction) is indicated by `__ARM_FEATURE_CLZ`.

name: __clsl
description: Returns the number of leading sign bits in `x`. When `x` is zero it returns the argument width - 1, that is, 31 or 63. These intrinsics are available on all targets. On targets without the CLZ instruction it should be implemented as an instruction sequence or a call to such a sequence. Fast hardware implementation (using a CLS instruction or a short code sequence involving the CLZ instruction) is indicated by `__ARM_FEATURE_CLZ`.

name: __clsll
description: Returns the number of leading sign bits in `x`. When `x` is zero it returns the argument width - 1, that is, 31 or 63. These intrinsics are available on all targets. On targets without the CLZ instruction it should be implemented as an instruction sequence or a call to such a sequence. Fast hardware implementation (using a CLS instruction or a short code sequence involving the CLZ instruction) is indicated by `__ARM_FEATURE_CLZ`.

name: __clz
description: Returns the number of leading zero bits in `x`. When `x` is zero it returns the argument width, that is, 32 or 64. These intrinsics are available on all targets. On targets without the CLZ instruction it should be implemented as an instruction sequence or a call to such a sequence. A suitable sequence can be found in [Warren](#Warren) (fig. 5-7). Hardware support for these intrinsics is indicated by `__ARM_FEATURE_CLZ`.

name: __clzl
description: Returns the number of leading zero bits in `x`. When `x` is zero it returns the argument width, that is, 32 or 64. These intrinsics are available on all targets. On targets without the CLZ instruction it should be implemented as an instruction sequence or a call to such a sequence. A suitable sequence can be found in [Warren](#Warren) (fig. 5-7). Hardware support for these intrinsics is indicated by `__ARM_FEATURE_CLZ`.

name: __clzll
description: Returns the number of leading zero bits in `x`. When `x` is zero it returns the argument width, that is, 32 or 64. These intrinsics are available on all targets. On targets without the CLZ instruction it should be implemented as an instruction sequence or a call to such a sequence. A suitable sequence can be found in [Warren](#Warren) (fig. 5-7). Hardware support for these intrinsics is indicated by `__ARM_FEATURE_CLZ`.

name: __crc32cd
description: Performs CRC-32C checksum from double words.  To access these intrinsics, `<arm_acle.h>` should be included.

name: __crc32d
description: Performs CRC-32 checksum from double words.

name: __dbg
description: Generates a DBG instruction. This provides a hint to debugging and related systems. The argument must be a constant integer from 0 to 15 inclusive. See implementation documentation for the effect (if any) of this instruction and the meaning of the argument. This is available only when compiling for AArch32.

name: __dmb
description: Generates a DMB (data memory barrier) instruction or equivalent CP15 instruction. DMB ensures the observed ordering of memory accesses. Memory accesses of the specified type issued before the DMB are guaranteed to be observed (in the specified scope) before memory accesses issued after the DMB. For example, DMB should be used between storing data, and updating a flag variable that makes that data available to another core.  The `__dmb()` intrinsic also acts as a compiler memory barrier of the appropriate type.

name: __dsb
description: Generates a DSB (data synchronization barrier) instruction or equivalent CP15 instruction. DSB ensures the completion of memory accesses. A DSB behaves as the equivalent DMB and has additional properties. After a DSB instruction completes, all memory accesses of the specified type issued before the DSB are guaranteed to have completed.  The `__dsb()` intrinsic also acts as a compiler memory barrier of the appropriate type.

name: __fma
prototypes: double __fma(double x, double y, double z);
float __fmaf(float x, float y, float z);
description: The `__fma` intrinsics compute `(x*y)+z`, without intermediate rounding. These intrinsics are available only if `__ARM_FEATURE_FMA` is defined. On a Standard C implementation it should not normally be necessary to use these intrinsics, because the fma functions defined in [C99] (7.12.13) should expand directly to the instructions if available.

name: __fmaf
prototypes: double __fma(double x, double y, double z);
float __fmaf(float x, float y, float z);
description: The `__fma` intrinsics compute `(x*y)+z`, without intermediate rounding. These intrinsics are available only if `__ARM_FEATURE_FMA` is defined. On a Standard C implementation it should not normally be necessary to use these intrinsics, because the fma functions defined in [C99] (7.12.13) should expand directly to the instructions if available.

name: __isb
description: Generates an ISB (instruction synchronization barrier) instruction or equivalent CP15 instruction. This instruction flushes the processor pipeline fetch buffers, so that following instructions are fetched from cache or memory. An ISB is needed after some system maintenance operations.  An ISB is also needed before transferring control to code that has been loaded or modified in memory, for example by an overlay mechanism or just-in-time code generator. (Note that if instruction and data caches are separate, privileged cache maintenance operations would be needed in order to unify the caches.)  The only supported argument for the `__isb()` intrinsic is 15, corresponding to the SY (full system) scope of the ISB instruction.

name: __jcvt
description: Converts a double-precision floating-point number to a 32-bit signed integer following the Javascript Convert instruction semantics [[ARMARMv83]](#ARMARMv83). The `__jcvt` intrinsic is available if `__ARM_FEATURE_JCVT` is defined.

name: __nop
description: Generates an unspecified no-op instruction. Note that not all architectures provide a distinguished NOP instruction. On those that do, it is unspecified whether this intrinsic generates it or another instruction. It is not guaranteed that inserting this instruction will increase execution time.

name: __pld
description: Generates a data prefetch instruction, if available. The argument should be any expression that may designate a data address. The data is prefetched to the innermost level of cache, for reading.

name: __pldir
description: Generates an intent to read on update prefetch instruction. The argument should be any expression that may designate a data address. This intrinsic does not require specification of cache level or retention policy. Support for this intrinsic is indicated by `__ARM_FEATURE_PCDPHINT`.

name: __pli
description: Generates a code prefetch instruction, if available. If a specific code prefetch instruction is not available, this intrinsic may generate a data-prefetch instruction to fetch the addressed code to the innermost level of unified cache. It will not fetch code to data-cache in a split cache level.

name: __qadd
description: Adds two 32-bit signed integers, with saturation. Sets the Q flag if the addition saturates.

name: __qadd16
description: 2x16-bit addition, saturated to the range `-2**15` to `2**15-1`.

name: __qadd8
description: 4x8-bit addition, saturated to the range `-2**7` to `2**7-1`.

name: __qasx
description: Exchanges halfwords of second operand, adds high halfwords and subtracts low halfwords, saturating in each case.

name: __qdbl
description: Doubles a signed 32-bit number, with saturation. `__qdbl(x)` is equal to `__qadd(x,x)` except that the argument x is evaluated only once. Sets the Q flag if the addition saturates.

name: __qsax
description: Exchanges halfwords of second operand, subtracts high halfwords and adds low halfwords, saturating in each case.

name: __qsub
description: Subtracts two 32-bit signed integers, with saturation. Sets the Q flag if the subtraction saturates.

name: __rbit
description: Reverses the bits in `x`. These intrinsics are only available on targets with the RBIT instruction.

name: __rbitl
description: Reverses the bits in `x`. These intrinsics are only available on targets with the RBIT instruction.

name: __rbitll
description: Reverses the bits in `x`. These intrinsics are only available on targets with the RBIT instruction.

name: __rev
description: Reverses the byte order within a word or doubleword. These intrinsics are available on all targets and should be expanded to an efficient straight-line code sequence on targets without byte reversal instructions.

name: __rev16
description: Reverses the byte order within each halfword of a word. For example, `0x12345678` becomes `0x34127856`. These intrinsics are available on all targets and should be expanded to an efficient straight-line code sequence on targets without byte reversal instructions.

name: __rev16l
description: Reverses the byte order within each halfword of a word. For example, `0x12345678` becomes `0x34127856`. These intrinsics are available on all targets and should be expanded to an efficient straight-line code sequence on targets without byte reversal instructions.

name: __rev16ll
description: Reverses the byte order within each halfword of a word. For example, `0x12345678` becomes `0x34127856`. These intrinsics are available on all targets and should be expanded to an efficient straight-line code sequence on targets without byte reversal instructions.

name: __revl
description: Reverses the byte order within a word or doubleword. These intrinsics are available on all targets and should be expanded to an efficient straight-line code sequence on targets without byte reversal instructions.

name: __revll
description: Reverses the byte order within a word or doubleword. These intrinsics are available on all targets and should be expanded to an efficient straight-line code sequence on targets without byte reversal instructions.

name: __revsh
description: Reverses the byte order in a 16-bit value and returns the signed 16-bit result. For example, `0x0080` becomes `0x8000`. This intrinsic is available on all targets and should be expanded to an efficient straight-line code sequence on targets without byte reversal instructions.

name: __rint32x
description: These intrinsics round their floating-point argument to a floating-point value that would be representable in a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer type. Out-of-Range values are forced to the Most Negative Integer representable in the target size, and an Invalid Operation Floating-Point Exception is generated.  The rounding mode can be either the ambient rounding mode (for example `__rint32xf`) or towards zero (for example `__rint32zf`).  These instructions are introduced in the Armv8.5-A extensions [[ARMARMv85]](#ARMARMv85) and are available only in the AArch64 execution state. The intrinsics are available when `__ARM_FEATURE_FRINT` is defined.

name: __rint32xf
description: These intrinsics round their floating-point argument to a floating-point value that would be representable in a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer type. Out-of-Range values are forced to the Most Negative Integer representable in the target size, and an Invalid Operation Floating-Point Exception is generated.  The rounding mode can be either the ambient rounding mode (for example `__rint32xf`) or towards zero (for example `__rint32zf`).  These instructions are introduced in the Armv8.5-A extensions [[ARMARMv85]](#ARMARMv85) and are available only in the AArch64 execution state. The intrinsics are available when `__ARM_FEATURE_FRINT` is defined.

name: __rint32z
description: These intrinsics round their floating-point argument to a floating-point value that would be representable in a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer type. Out-of-Range values are forced to the Most Negative Integer representable in the target size, and an Invalid Operation Floating-Point Exception is generated.  The rounding mode can be either the ambient rounding mode (for example `__rint32xf`) or towards zero (for example `__rint32zf`).  These instructions are introduced in the Armv8.5-A extensions [[ARMARMv85]](#ARMARMv85) and are available only in the AArch64 execution state. The intrinsics are available when `__ARM_FEATURE_FRINT` is defined.

name: __rint32zf
description: These intrinsics round their floating-point argument to a floating-point value that would be representable in a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer type. Out-of-Range values are forced to the Most Negative Integer representable in the target size, and an Invalid Operation Floating-Point Exception is generated.  The rounding mode can be either the ambient rounding mode (for example `__rint32xf`) or towards zero (for example `__rint32zf`).  These instructions are introduced in the Armv8.5-A extensions [[ARMARMv85]](#ARMARMv85) and are available only in the AArch64 execution state. The intrinsics are available when `__ARM_FEATURE_FRINT` is defined.

name: __rint64x
description: These intrinsics round their floating-point argument to a floating-point value that would be representable in a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer type. Out-of-Range values are forced to the Most Negative Integer representable in the target size, and an Invalid Operation Floating-Point Exception is generated.  The rounding mode can be either the ambient rounding mode (for example `__rint32xf`) or towards zero (for example `__rint32zf`).  These instructions are introduced in the Armv8.5-A extensions [[ARMARMv85]](#ARMARMv85) and are available only in the AArch64 execution state. The intrinsics are available when `__ARM_FEATURE_FRINT` is defined.

name: __rint64xf
description: These intrinsics round their floating-point argument to a floating-point value that would be representable in a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer type. Out-of-Range values are forced to the Most Negative Integer representable in the target size, and an Invalid Operation Floating-Point Exception is generated.  The rounding mode can be either the ambient rounding mode (for example `__rint32xf`) or towards zero (for example `__rint32zf`).  These instructions are introduced in the Armv8.5-A extensions [[ARMARMv85]](#ARMARMv85) and are available only in the AArch64 execution state. The intrinsics are available when `__ARM_FEATURE_FRINT` is defined.

name: __rint64z
description: These intrinsics round their floating-point argument to a floating-point value that would be representable in a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer type. Out-of-Range values are forced to the Most Negative Integer representable in the target size, and an Invalid Operation Floating-Point Exception is generated.  The rounding mode can be either the ambient rounding mode (for example `__rint32xf`) or towards zero (for example `__rint32zf`).  These instructions are introduced in the Armv8.5-A extensions [[ARMARMv85]](#ARMARMv85) and are available only in the AArch64 execution state. The intrinsics are available when `__ARM_FEATURE_FRINT` is defined.

name: __rint64zf
description: These intrinsics round their floating-point argument to a floating-point value that would be representable in a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer type. Out-of-Range values are forced to the Most Negative Integer representable in the target size, and an Invalid Operation Floating-Point Exception is generated.  The rounding mode can be either the ambient rounding mode (for example `__rint32xf`) or towards zero (for example `__rint32zf`).  These instructions are introduced in the Armv8.5-A extensions [[ARMARMv85]](#ARMARMv85) and are available only in the AArch64 execution state. The intrinsics are available when `__ARM_FEATURE_FRINT` is defined.

name: __rintn
description: The `__rintn` intrinsics perform a floating point round to integral, to nearest with ties to even. The `__rintn` intrinsic is available when `__ARM_FEATURE_DIRECTED_ROUNDING` is defined to 1. For other rounding modes like |lsquo| to nearest with ties to away |rsquo| it is strongly recommended that C99 standard functions be used. To achieve a floating point convert to integer, rounding to |lsquo| nearest with ties to even |rsquo| operation, use these rounding functions with a type-cast to integral values. For example:

name: __rintnf
description: The `__rintn` intrinsics perform a floating point round to integral, to nearest with ties to even. The `__rintn` intrinsic is available when `__ARM_FEATURE_DIRECTED_ROUNDING` is defined to 1. For other rounding modes like |lsquo| to nearest with ties to away |rsquo| it is strongly recommended that C99 standard functions be used. To achieve a floating point convert to integer, rounding to |lsquo| nearest with ties to even |rsquo| operation, use these rounding functions with a type-cast to integral values. For example:

name: __rndr
description: Stores a 64-bit random number into the object pointed to by the argument and returns zero. If the implementation could not generate a random number  within a reasonable period of time the object pointed to by the input is set to zero and a non-zero value is returned.

name: __rndrrs
description: Reseeds the random number generator.  After that stores a 64-bit random number into the object pointed to by the argument and returns zero. If the implementation could not generate a random number within a reasonable period of time the object pointed to by the input is set to zero and a non-zero value is returned.  These intrinsics have side-effects on the system beyond their results. Implementations must preserve them even if the results of the intrinsics are unused.  To access these intrinsics, `<arm_acle.h>` should be included.

name: __ror
prototypes: unsigned long __rorl(unsigned long x, uint32_t y);
description: Rotates the argument `x` right by `y` bits. `y` can take any value. These intrinsics are available on all targets.

name: __rorl
prototypes: unsigned long __rorl(unsigned long x, uint32_t y);
description: Rotates the argument `x` right by `y` bits. `y` can take any value. These intrinsics are available on all targets.

name: __rorll
prototypes: unsigned long __rorl(unsigned long x, uint32_t y);
description: Rotates the argument `x` right by `y` bits. `y` can take any value. These intrinsics are available on all targets.

name: __sadd16
description: 2x16-bit signed addition. The GE bits are set according to the results.

name: __sadd8
description: 4x8-bit signed addition. The GE bits are set according to the results.

name: __sasx
description: Exchanges halfwords of the second operand, adds high halfwords and subtracts low halfwords. The GE bits are set according to the results.

name: __sel
description: Selects each byte of the result from either the first operand or the second operand, according to the values of the GE bits. For each result byte, if the corresponding GE bit is set then the byte from the first operand is used, otherwise the byte from the second operand is used. Because of the way that `int16x2_t` operations set two (duplicate) GE bits per value, the `__sel` intrinsic works equally well on `(u)int16x2_t` and `(u)int8x4_t` data.

name: __sev
description: Generates a SEV (send a global event) hint instruction. This causes an event to be signaled to all processors in a multiprocessor system. It is a NOP on a uniprocessor system.

name: __sevl
description: Generates a send a local event hint instruction. This causes an event to be signaled to only the processor executing this instruction. In a multiprocessor system, it is not required to affect the other processors.

name: __shasx
description: Exchanges halfwords of the second operand, adds high halfwords and subtract low halfwords, halving the results.

name: __shsax
description: Exchanges halfwords of the second operand, subtracts high halfwords and add low halfwords, halving the results.

name: __smlabb
description: Multiplies two 16-bit signed integers, the low halfwords of the first two operands, and adds to the third operand. Sets the Q flag if the addition overflows. (Note that the addition is the usual 32-bit modulo addition which wraps on overflow, not a saturating addition. The multiplication cannot overflow.)

name: __smlabt
description: Multiplies the low halfword of the first operand and the high halfword of the second operand, and adds to the third operand, as for `__smlabb`.

name: __smlad
description: Performs 2x16-bit multiplication and adds both results to the third operand. Sets the Q flag if the addition overflows. (Overflow cannot occur during the multiplications.)

name: __smladx
description: Exchanges the halfwords of the second operand, performs 2x16-bit multiplication, and adds both results to the third operand. Sets the Q flag if the addition overflows. (Overflow cannot occur during the multiplications.)

name: __smlald
description: Performs 2x16-bit multiplication and adds both results to the 64-bit third operand. Overflow in the addition is not detected.

name: __smlaldx
description: Exchanges the halfwords of the second operand, performs 2x16-bit multiplication and adds both results to the 64-bit third operand. Overflow in the addition is not detected.

name: __smlatb
description: Multiplies the high halfword of the first operand and the low halfword of the second operand, and adds to the third operand, as for `__smlabb`.

name: __smlatt
description: Multiplies the high halfwords of the first two operands and adds to the third operand, as for `__smlabb`.

name: __smlawb
description: Multiplies the 32-bit signed first operand with the low halfword (as a 16-bit signed integer) of the second operand. Adds the top 32 bits of the 48-bit product to the third operand. Sets the Q flag if the addition overflows. (See note for `__smlabb`).

name: __smlawt
description: Multiplies the 32-bit signed first operand with the high halfword (as a 16-bit signed integer) of the second operand and adds the top 32 bits of the 48-bit result to the third operand as for `__smlawb`.

name: __smlsd
description: Performs two 16-bit signed multiplications. Takes the difference of the products, subtracting the high-halfword product from the low-halfword product, and adds the difference to the third operand. Sets the Q flag if the addition overflows. (Overflow cannot occur during the multiplications or the subtraction.)

name: __smlsdx
description: Performs two 16-bit signed multiplications. The product of the high halfword of the first operand and the low halfword of the second operand is subtracted from the product of the low halfword of the first operand and the high halfword of the second operand, and the difference is added to the third operand. Sets the Q flag if the addition overflows. (Overflow cannot occur during the multiplications or the subtraction.)

name: __smlsld
description: Perform two 16-bit signed multiplications. Take the difference of the products, subtracting the high-halfword product from the low-halfword product, and add the difference to the third operand. Overflow in the 64-bit addition is not detected. (Overflow cannot occur during the multiplications or the subtraction.)

name: __smlsldx
description: Perform two 16-bit signed multiplications. The product of the high halfword of the first operand and the low halfword of the second operand is subtracted from the product of the low halfword of the first operand and the high halfword of the second operand, and the difference is added to the third operand. Overflow in the 64-bit addition is not detected. (Overflow cannot occur during the multiplications or the subtraction.)

name: __smuad
description: Perform 2x16-bit signed multiplications, adding the products together. Set the Q flag if the addition overflows.

name: __smuadx
description: Exchange the halfwords of the second operand (or equivalently, the first operand), perform 2x16-bit signed multiplications, and add the products together. Set the Q flag if the addition overflows.

name: __smulbb
description: Multiplies two 16-bit signed integers, that is, the low halfwords of the operands.

name: __smulbt
description: Multiplies the low halfword of the first operand and the high halfword of the second operand.

name: __smultb
description: Multiplies the high halfword of the first operand and the low halfword of the second operand.

name: __smultt
description: Multiplies the high halfwords of the operands.

name: __smulwb
description: Multiplies the 32-bit signed first operand with the low halfword (as a 16-bit signed integer) of the second operand. Return the top 32 bits of the 48-bit product.

name: __smulwt
description: Multiplies the 32-bit signed first operand with the high halfword (as a 16-bit signed integer) of the second operand. Return the top 32 bits of the 48-bit product.

name: __smusd
description: Perform two 16-bit signed multiplications. Take the difference of the products, subtracting the high-halfword product from the low-halfword product.

name: __smusdx
description: Perform two 16-bit signed multiplications. The product of the high halfword of the first operand and the low halfword of the second operand is subtracted from the product of the low halfword of the first operand and the high halfword of the second operand.

name: __sqrt
description: The `__sqrt` intrinsics compute the square root of their operand. They have no effect on errno. Negative values produce a default NaN result and possible floating-point exception as described in [ARMARM] (A2.7.7).

name: __sqrtf
description: The `__sqrt` intrinsics compute the square root of their operand. They have no effect on errno. Negative values produce a default NaN result and possible floating-point exception as described in [ARMARM] (A2.7.7).

name: __ssat
description: Saturates a signed integer to the given bit width in the range 1 to 32. For example, the result of saturation to 8-bit width will be in the range -128 to 127. The Q flag is set if the operation saturates.

name: __ssat16
description: Saturates two 16-bit signed values to a width in the range 1 to 16. The Q flag is set if either operation saturates.

name: __ssax
description: Exchanges halfwords of the second operand, subtracts high halfwords and adds low halfwords. The GE bits are set according to the results.

name: __ssub16
description: 2x16-bit signed subtraction. The GE bits are set according to the results.

name: __ssub8
description: 4x8-bit signed subtraction. The GE bits are set according to the results.

name: __swp
description: Unconditionally stores a new value at the given address, and returns the old value.  As with the IA-64/GCC primitives described in 0, the `__swp` intrinsic is polymorphic. The second argument must provide the address of a byte-sized object or an aligned word-sized object and it must be possible to determine the size of this object from the argument expression.  This intrinsic is implemented by LDREX/STREX (or LDREXB/STREXB) where available, as if by

name: __sxtab16
description: Two values (at bit positions 0..7 and 16..23) are extracted from the second operand, sign-extended to 16 bits, and added to the first operand.

name: __sxtb16
description: Two values (at bit positions 0..7 and 16..23) are extracted from the first operand, sign-extended to 16 bits, and returned as the result.

name: __uadd16
description: 2x16-bit unsigned addition. The GE bits are set according to the results.

name: __uadd8
description: 4x8-bit unsigned addition. The GE bits are set according to the results.

name: __uasx
description: Exchanges halfwords of the second operand, adds high halfwords and subtracts low halfwords. The GE bits are set according to the results of unsigned addition.

name: __uhasx
description: Exchanges halfwords of the second operand, adds high halfwords and subtracts low halfwords, halving the results.

name: __uhsax
description: Exchanges halfwords of the second operand, subtracts high halfwords and adds low halfwords, halving the results.

name: __uqadd16
description: 2x16-bit unsigned addition, saturating to the range `0` to `2**16-1`.

name: __uqadd8
description: 4x8-bit unsigned addition, saturating to the range `0` to `2**8-1`.

name: __uqasx
description: Exchanges halfwords of the second operand, and performs saturating unsigned addition on the high halfwords and saturating unsigned subtraction on the low halfwords.

name: __uqsax
description: Exchanges halfwords of the second operand, and performs saturating unsigned subtraction on the high halfwords and saturating unsigned addition on the low halfwords.

name: __uqsub16
description: 2x16-bit unsigned subtraction, saturating to the range `0` to `2**16-1`.

name: __uqsub8
description: 4x8-bit unsigned subtraction, saturating to the range `0` to `2**8-1`.

name: __usad8
description: Performs 4x8-bit unsigned subtraction, and adds the absolute values of the differences together, returning the result as a single unsigned integer.

name: __usada8
description: Performs 4x8-bit unsigned subtraction, adds the absolute values of the differences together, and adds the result to the third operand.

name: __usat
description: Saturates a signed integer to an unsigned (non-negative) integer of a bit width in the range 0 to 31. For example, the result of saturation to 8-bit width is in the range 0 to 255, with all negative inputs going to zero. The Q flag is set if the operation saturates.

name: __usat16
description: Saturates two 16-bit signed values to a bit width in the range 0 to 15. The input values are signed and the output values are non-negative, with all negative inputs going to zero. The Q flag is set if either operation saturates.

name: __usax
description: Exchanges the halfwords of the second operand, subtracts the high halfwords and adds the low halfwords. Sets the GE bits according to the results of unsigned addition.

name: __usub16
description: 2x16-bit unsigned subtraction. The GE bits are set according to the results.

name: __usub8
description: 4x8-bit unsigned subtraction. The GE bits are set according to the results.

name: __uxtab16
description: Two values (at bit positions 0..7 and 16..23) are extracted from the second operand, zero-extended to 16 bits, and added to the first operand.

name: __uxtb16
description: Two values (at bit positions 0..7 and 16..23) are extracted from the first operand, zero-extended to 16 bits, and returned as the result.

name: __wfe
description: Generates a WFE (wait for event) hint instruction, or nothing. The WFE instruction allows (but does not require) the processor to enter a low-power state until some event occurs such as a SEV being issued by another processor.

name: __wfi
description: Generates a WFI (wait for interrupt) hint instruction, or nothing. The WFI instruction allows (but does not require) the processor to enter a low-power state until one of a number of asynchronous events occurs.

name: __yield
description: Generates a YIELD hint instruction. This enables multithreading software to indicate to the hardware that it is performing a task, for example a spin-lock, that could be swapped out to improve overall system performance.

name: svset2
prototypes: svboolx2_t svset2[_b](svboolx2_t tuple, uint64_t imm_index, svbool_t x);
description: When `__ARM_FEATURE_SVE2p1` is defined, [`<arm_sve.h>`](#arm_sve.h) defines the tuple types `svboolx2_t` and `svboolx4_t`.  These are opaque tuple types that can be accessed using the SVE intrinsics `svsetN`, `svgetN` and `svcreateN`. `svundef2` and `svundef4` are also extended to work with `svboolx2_t` and `svboolx4_t`.  For example:

prototypes: svboolx2_t svset2[_b](svboolx2_t tuple, uint64_t imm_index, svbool_t x);

name: svset_neonq
description: These intrinsics set the first 128 bits of SVE vector `vec` to `subvec`.  That is, bit *i* of the result is equal to: * bit *i* of `subvec` if *i* < 128 * bit *i* of `vec` otherwise  On big-endian targets, this leaves lanes in a different order from the “native” SVE order.  For example, if `subvec` is `int32x4_t`, then on big-endian targets, the first memory element is in lane 3 of `subvec` and is therefore in lane 3 of the returned SVE vector.  Using `svld1` to load elements would instead put the first memory element in lane 0 of the returned SVE vector.  When `svundef` is passed as the `vec` parameter, compilers are able to reuse the SVE register overlapping the NEON input without generating additional instructions.

name: svundef2_b
prototypes: svboolx2_t svset2[_b](svboolx2_t tuple, uint64_t imm_index, svbool_t x);
description: When `__ARM_FEATURE_SVE2p1` is defined, [`<arm_sve.h>`](#arm_sve.h) defines the tuple types `svboolx2_t` and `svboolx4_t`.  These are opaque tuple types that can be accessed using the SVE intrinsics `svsetN`, `svgetN` and `svcreateN`. `svundef2` and `svundef4` are also extended to work with `svboolx2_t` and `svboolx4_t`.  For example:

name: svundef_za
full_prototype: void svundef_za() __arm_streaming_compatible;
